primary_diagnosis.name: Oligoarticular psoriatic arthritis flare (dactylitis + knee effusion) — septic arthritis to be excluded by aspiration

pathophysiology_summary: Oligoarticular psoriatic arthritis produces asymmetric involvement of a few joints, with dactylitis (whole-digit tenosynovitis/'sausage digit'), enthesitis and large-joint effusions, on a background of psoriasis and nail disease (pitting). This presentation — a sausage finger plus a warm knee effusion with nail pitting — is highly characteristic of PsA. However, a warm, fluid-filled joint with even low-grade fever is a 'hot joint' that must be aspirated to exclude septic arthritis, which rapidly destroys cartilage. Reassuring features are simultaneous asymmetric two-site involvement, dactylitis and nail changes, no single fiery/tense knee or high fever/rigors, no skin portal of entry, and no immunosuppression — but none of these excludes infection. Telehealth cannot examine or aspirate the joint, so the correct action is urgent same-day in-person assessment with knee aspiration (± synovial analysis) rather than remote high-dose NSAIDs, with septic-joint safety-netting and attention to self-care (dominant hand + knee affected while living alone).

differential.evidence_basis: Dactylitis + asymmetric knee effusion + nail pitting.
A warm effused knee with fever must be aspirated to exclude infection.
Crystal arthritis possible; dactylitis/nail changes favour PsA — aspiration would settle it.
No preceding infection; established psoriasis favours PsA.

differential.diagnosis: Oligoarticular psoriatic arthritis (septic joint to exclude)
differential.evidence_basis: Characteristic PsA features with no fiery/tense knee or rigors — but the hot joint still needs aspiration.
Lower probability but not excludable without aspiration.
No preceding infection; PsA features present.

differential.diagnosis: Oligoarticular PsA — septic arthritis excluded by aspiration
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth position: likely oligoarticular PsA, but urgent same-day in-person assessment with knee aspiration is required before this is accepted.

red_flags.description: A warm, fluid-filled ('hot') joint — must be aspirated to exclude septic arthritis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Septic arthritis rapidly destroys cartilage and causes sepsis; a hot joint requires aspiration regardless of a likely inflammatory cause.

red_flags.description: Knee becoming fiery red, tense and unbearably hot, or high fever with rigors
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would strongly indicate joint sepsis needing emergency care.

red_flags.description: Loss of hand/knee function while living alone (self-care risk)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Dominant-hand and knee involvement impairs food preparation and safe mobility.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the warm knee effusion to PsA without aspirating to exclude septic arthritis.
Prescribing high-dose NSAIDs remotely instead of arranging joint assessment/aspiration.
Offering phone-only reassurance for a warm effused joint.
Not addressing loss of dominant-hand/knee function while isolated.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Warm effused knee with low-grade fever; no fiery/tense joint or rigors — aspiration required to exclude sepsis
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports urgent same-day in-person assessment with knee aspiration.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Joint examination and aspiration/synovial fluid analysis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment with knee aspiration (± synovial analysis) to exclude septic arthritis; take bloods; refer to rheumatology; escalate to ED for a fiery/tense hot knee or high fever with rigors, and arrange self-care support.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing a warm effused joint remotely as PsA could miss septic arthritis and cause rapid joint destruction.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: An acutely warm, swollen joint requires aspiration to exclude septic arthritis before treating as inflammatory/crystal arthritis.
PsA is treated with NSAIDs, local steroid injection and DMARD/biologic therapy under specialist guidance once infection is excluded.
